reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong use of comparison tables — the 'Combining security guardrails' summary table and the 'Guardrails vs alarms' side-by-side table are genuinely useful for decision-making and will be cited well by AI engines.
Each guardrail section follows a consistent pattern: what it enforces, when to enable, when to skip, and the exact error message. This predictable rhythm makes the page easy to scan and reference.

FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS behavior, choosing/comparison) and includes genuinely useful entries like 'What is VPC-only database accessibility?' that serve a low-AWS-experience audience.

The 'When NOT to use guardrails' section is correctly placed as a peer H2 — not nested under 'When to use' — and gives a concrete, non-circular reason to skip guardrails (early prototyping, solo developer).
Error message code blocks for each guardrail give readers a concrete failure signature, which is exactly what a developer needs to debug a blocked deployment.
problems: MANDATORY PRICING VIOLATION: The FAQ answer for 'What is the cost impact of enabling these guardrails?' invents specific dollar prices not present in any provided source file: '~$32/month per AZ' for NAT gateways, '~$5/month base + $0.60/million requests' for WAF, '$0.50/month each' for hosted zones. The pricing rules state these must not be invented. This undermines reader trust.

COMPLETENESS GAP — 6 of 15 guardrail types in guardrails.d.ts are completely absent with no acknowledgment: function-memory-limit, function-timeout-limit, container-resource-limit, database-engine-restriction, database-instance-restriction, resource-count-limit. Even if these belong on a separate page, the reader has no pointer to them. A reader who reads this page and also the referenced '/guardrails/deployment' page would still have no idea these guardrail types exist.

The claim 'Enforced on four commands — deploy, codebuild:deploy, preview-changes, and delete' is not verifiable from the provided source file (guardrails.d.ts only defines schema shapes, not enforcement logic). If this is correct, it should be noted as coming from CLI behavior. If incorrect, it will mislead readers.

The 'require-vpc-databases' section states 'Every database must have its accessibilityMode set to vpc or scoping-workloads-in-vpc' — the specific mode names are not present in the source JSDoc, which only says 'VPC-only accessibility'. This is a source-grounding issue for the exact string values.

The SQS dead-letter queue example uses redrivePolicy.deadLetterQueue: 'deadLetterQueue' as a plain string without explaining this is a resource name reference (not a string literal). A first-time user won't understand this syntax.
mandatoryFixes: Remove all invented dollar-amount AWS pricing from the FAQ answer 'What is the cost impact of enabling these guardrails?'. Replace with pricing-model descriptions only (e.g., 'NAT gateways incur an hourly charge per AZ plus data transfer costs — check AWS pricing for current rates') or remove the cost-specific sentences entirely. The rule is: never quote specific AWS prices not present in the provided source files.

Add a cross-reference section or callout acknowledging the guardrail types not covered on this page (function-memory-limit, function-timeout-limit, container-resource-limit, database-engine-restriction, database-instance-restriction, resource-count-limit). Even a single sentence — 'Stacktape also includes resource-limit guardrails for Lambda memory and timeout, container CPU/memory, database engine selection, and stack resource counts. These are covered on the [Resource Limits Guardrails](/guardrails/resource-limits) page.' — is enough to prevent the reader from thinking the list is exhaustive.
optionalImprovements: In the SQS dead-letter queue example, add a one-sentence prose note explaining that 'deadLetterQueue' is the name of the DLQ resource defined earlier in the same config — without this, the string value looks like a magic constant.

Soften or qualify the four-command enforcement claim ('deploy, codebuild:deploy, preview-changes, and delete') with a note that this reflects CLI behavior, or link to a source that confirms it, since the provided types file cannot verify it.

The 'require-custom-domain' section says 'auto-generated AWS URLs change across redeployments' — this is worth verifying; ALB DNS names are stable across deployments for the same load balancer, so this claim may be inaccurate for web services (though it may be accurate for other resource types).

The page references '/guardrails/deployment' multiple times but never introduces what that page covers. A one-sentence description ('The deployment guardrails page covers stage, region, and command restrictions') would orient the reader without requiring them to click through.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Per-guardrail structure with concrete when-to-enable and when-to-skip guidance is exactly what this audience needs — each section delivers a usable decision, not just configuration.
The 'Guardrails vs alarms' table is precise and well-grounded: preventive vs reactive, pre-deployment vs runtime, blocking vs notification. Prevents a common conceptual conflation.
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS mechanics, comparison/decision) with production-relevant Q&A pairs like NAT gateway costs and accidental deletion protection.
Code examples follow the correct <CodeBlock intellisense> format with defineConfig and class-based resources. No YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type imports.
The DLQ example showing deadLetterQueue as a string resource reference alongside maxReceiveCount is realistic and copyable — a reader can adapt it directly.
The 'Combining security guardrails' summary table is efficient and scannable — it tells a platform engineer exactly which guardrails map to which production concerns without repeating all the prose.
problems: FACTUAL CONCERN: The 'require-vpc-databases' section states the guardrail applies to 'all relational databases and OpenSearch domains'. The source JSDoc for RequireVpcDatabasesGuardrail says only 'all databases must use VPC-only accessibility' — it does not enumerate OpenSearch. The guardrail type is literally named 'require-vpc-databases'. Claiming OpenSearch is in scope is not grounded in the provided source. An engineer relying on this guardrail to isolate OpenSearch may be misled.

UNGROUNDED: 'Enforced on four commands — deploy, codebuild:deploy, preview-changes, and delete' is not present in the source file. The source only defines guardrail types and properties. This is a significant claim for an engineer trying to understand when guardrail checks fire.

UNGROUNDED: 'Organization-scoped — configured once in the Stacktape Console' is not in the source file. The source defines guardrail types only; it says nothing about Console configuration, organization-level scoping, or whether project-level overrides are possible. This architectural claim should be narrowed or flagged as source-unverifiable.

COMPLETENESS GAP: The source defines 'database-engine-restriction' in the same guardrails.d.ts file. This guardrail has direct security/compliance applications (e.g., blocking unapproved or unencrypted engine versions) and is absent from this page. An engineer enforcing 'only use Postgres 16+' for security reasons will not find it here.

UNGROUNDED: Specific error message strings (e.g., 'Database "mainDatabase" has accessibility mode "internet". All databases must use VPC-only accessibility (vpc or scoping-workloads-in-vpc).') are not present in any source file provided. These may be accurate, but presenting them as exact error output without source grounding sets incorrect expectations. An engineer will notice if the actual message differs.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or qualify the claim that 'require-vpc-databases' applies to OpenSearch domains. The source JSDoc covers only 'all databases' and the guardrail is named 'require-vpc-databases'. Either ground the OpenSearch claim in a source file that enumerates it explicitly, or remove the mention. A statement like 'applies to relational databases; OpenSearch domain applicability is not documented at this layer' is safer than an ungrounded assertion that may mislead engineers configuring network isolation.

Add coverage for the 'database-engine-restriction' guardrail, which is defined in the same guardrails.d.ts source file alongside the five guardrails already covered. It has clear security/compliance applications (block deprecated or insecure engine versions, enforce approved engine lists). Omitting it from a 'security and data protection' page creates a completeness gap that matters to compliance engineers.
optionalImprovements: Either source-ground or remove the 'four commands' enforcement claim (deploy, codebuild:deploy, preview-changes, delete). If grounded in another source file not provided, cite it. If uncertain, replace with 'enforced during deployment operations; check the CLI reference for per-command behavior'.
Qualify the Console/organization-scoping claim with a hedge or link to Console docs, since the source file only defines types and makes no statements about how guardrails are configured or scoped.

The error message blocks read as exact system output. Prefix them with 'similar to' or 'along the lines of' to set accurate expectations without claiming the strings are literal, since they are not in the provided source.

The 'resource-type-restriction (security use)' section is more of a cross-reference pointer than a section. Consider either folding it into the combining table or removing it — its current form provides less value than the dedicated deployment guardrails page it points to, and it breaks the parallel structure of the other guardrail sections.

Consider adding 'database-instance-restriction' to the scope of this page (or at least the combining table) since blocking oversized instances can be relevant to budget governance that affects data protection posture (e.g., preventing production-scale instances in development environments that might hold real data).

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page has a clear decision-oriented structure: how guardrails work, when to use them, individual guardrails, combinations, guardrails vs alarms, and FAQ.
The Stacktape configuration examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/> with a visible TypeScript tab, avoiding the forbidden YAML and StacktapeConfig patterns.
The page correctly distinguishes preventive guardrails from reactive alarms at a high level, which matches the section instruction and helps readers choose the right mechanism.
The tables for combining guardrails and comparing guardrails vs alarms make the page easy to scan.
problems: Many Stacktape-specific claims are not grounded in the provided source file. guardrails.d.ts defines guardrail types and properties only; it does not state that guardrails are configured in the Stacktape Console, organization-scoped, pre-deployment, validated before CloudFormation, enforced on exactly four commands, or skipped by dev mode.
The page invents exact violation error messages. The provided source contains no runtime validation messages, so every quoted error block is unsupported.

The page over-specifies require-vpc-databases. The source says all databases must use VPC-only accessibility, but it does not define relational database/OpenSearch coverage, the accepted values vpc and scoping-workloads-in-vpc, or the default internet accessibility mode.

The page adds unsupported behavior for require-custom-domain, including customDomains array shape, generated AWS URLs changing across redeployments, TLS certificate control, and HTTPS setup. The source only says public-facing web services and hosting buckets must have a custom domain configured.

The FAQ includes invented concrete pricing for NAT gateways, WAF, and hosted zones, despite no pricing source being provided. This violates the pricing rule to never invent prices not present in source files.

The resource-type restriction section makes speculative security recommendations about blocking OpenSearch, Redis, and bastions, including an unsupported claim about Redis encryption at rest on older instance types.

The code examples rely on resource classes, properties, and engine classes that are not present in the provided source list. They may be valid Stacktape APIs elsewhere, but this review can only ground them in guardrails.d.ts.

Several AWS/security claims are phrased as Stacktape-specific behavior without source support, such as deletion protection retaining databases on stack deletion and no infrastructure changes being made after a guardrail failure.

The page does not cover several security/data-protection-relevant guardrails from the source, including database-engine-restriction, database-instance-restriction, function-memory-limit, function-timeout-limit, container-resource-limit, and resource-count-limit. Some may belong elsewhere, but the page title and route imply broader security/data-protection coverage than the current selection.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground the unsupported Console, organization-scope, command-enforcement, pre-CloudFormation, and exact failure-behavior claims. If those facts are required, add authoritative source files such as CLI/runtime guardrail validation code or Console source.

Delete the invented error-message code blocks, or replace them with cautious prose such as “deployment is blocked when the configured guardrail is violated” unless runtime validator source provides the exact messages.

Rewrite require-vpc-databases to match guardrails.d.ts narrowly: when enabled, all databases must use VPC-only accessibility with no public internet access. Do not name accepted accessibilityMode values, defaults, relational/OpenSearch coverage, or DynamoDB exclusions unless those facts are sourced.
Remove invented pricing figures from the FAQ. Keep only non-priced tradeoff language unless a provided pricing source contains those exact costs.

Remove unsupported resource-type security examples or rewrite them as generic examples directly grounded in the JSDoc: blockedResourceTypes can include resource type identifiers such as open-search-domain or redis-cluster.

Either remove the Stacktape resource configuration examples or add the relevant resource type source files that ground RelationalDatabase, SqsQueue, engine classes, accessibility, deletionProtection, and redrivePolicy.

Expand coverage or narrow the page title/scope. If this remains “Security and Data Protection Guardrails,” include the other relevant guardrail types from guardrails.d.ts, especially database-engine-restriction, database-instance-restriction, function limits, container limits, and resource-count-limit, with cautious source-grounded wording.

Correct the FAQ answer about stack deletion. guardrails.d.ts only says require-deletion-protection requires relational databases to set deletionProtection true; it does not state retention behavior or interaction with stack deletion.
optionalImprovements: Add a compact table listing every GuardrailType from guardrails.d.ts, its property key, and what it blocks. This would improve source coverage and make the page more useful for answer extraction.

Make H2 openings slightly more source-grounded and less operationally specific unless runtime sources are added. For example, “The require-waf guardrail has an enabled flag and applies to application load balancers” is safer than describing attachment internals.
Replace compliance-framework examples like HIPAA and PCI-DSS with broader wording unless a compliance source exists. The examples are plausible but not grounded in the provided file.
Consider splitting broad governance mechanics into a separate guardrails overview page if source files are available, and keep this page focused on security/data-protection guardrail types.

The guardrail list and several high-level guardrail descriptions match the source, but much of the operational behavior needs to be narrowed or removed unless additional source files are provided.

issues.statement: "these guardrails run before any infrastructure changes reach AWS"; "validated before CloudFormation operations begin"; "No infrastructure changes are made"; "deployment fails immediately with a clear error message."
issues.reason: The source only declares guardrail configuration types. It does not specify validation timing, CloudFormation ordering, rollback behavior, atomicity, or the exact user-facing failure behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Say guardrails are intended to block configurations that violate policy, but avoid claiming exact deployment timing, CloudFormation sequencing, or no-change guarantees unless runtime validator sources are provided.

issues.statement: "Enforced on four commands — `deploy`, `codebuild:deploy`, `preview-changes`, and `delete`. Other commands like `dev` do not enforce guardrails."
issues.reason: The guardrails type source does not state which CLI commands enforce guardrails. It only defines a command-restriction guardrail with a configurable `blockedCommands` list.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the enforcement-command list, or replace it with only the sourced fact that `command-restriction` can define `blockedCommands`. Add CLI/runtime sources if exact command enforcement needs to be documented.

issues.statement: The `require-vpc-databases` section says it applies to relational databases and OpenSearch domains, blocks the default `internet` accessibility mode, and allows exactly `vpc` or `scoping-workloads-in-vpc`.

issues.suggestedFix: Use the exact sourced wording: when enabled, all databases must use VPC-only accessibility with no public internet access. Remove OpenSearch, default `internet`, and exact enum claims unless the relevant resource type files are provided.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Correctly and crisply distinguishes guardrails (preventive, deployment-time) from alarms (reactive, runtime) with a comparison table — a genuine conceptual win for readers new to governance tooling.
Per-guardrail sections each carry a 'When to enable / When the default is fine' treatment, giving readers decision context rather than just configuration instructions.
FAQ is broad and well-structured, mixing Stacktape-specific, AWS-concept, and choosing/comparison questions as required.
The 'Combining security guardrails' summary table is a useful quick-reference for teams deciding on a production baseline.
No forbidden style violations detected: no raw YAML resource blocks, no StacktapeConfig import, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no marketing language.
The 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' sections are correctly structured as peer H2s.
CodeBlock examples correctly use `intellisense` and the TypeScript tab label, with imports hidden by focus markers.
problems: CRITICAL — The page never shows how to actually define a guardrail in a Stacktape config. Every code example shows what a *compliant resource* looks like, not the guardrail policy itself. A developer reading this page from start to finish would know guardrails exist and what they enforce, but would have no idea how to add one to their stacktape.ts (e.g., the `{ type: 'require-vpc-databases', properties: { enabled: true } }` entry in the config). This is the most important practical omission on the page.

The 'Require WAF' section has no code example — it is the only guardrail section that describes the requirement and tradeoffs but does not show either the guardrail definition or a compliant resource configuration, making it inconsistent with every other guardrail section.
The 'Require custom domain' section similarly omits a code example showing a compliant resource, breaking the pattern set by other sections.

There is no `<ApiReference>` component anywhere on the page. The source defines `GuardrailDefinition` and all its variant types — an API reference anchor would help readers who want to look up exact property names after reading the prose.

The examples for 'Require VPC databases' and 'Require deletion protection' show identical RelationalDatabase configs with the same `instanceSize: 'db.t4g.micro'` comment, which is only tangentially related to the guardrail being explained. The surrounding prose explaining that `instanceSize` comment dilutes the section's focus.
mandatoryFixes: Add at least one complete guardrail configuration example early in the page (ideally under 'How security guardrails work' or a new 'Basic example' section) showing how guardrails are defined in stacktape.ts — e.g., a defineConfig that returns a `guardrails` array with one or two entries like `{ type: 'require-vpc-databases', properties: { enabled: true } }`. Without this, the reader has no actionable path from reading to deploying. This is the single most important fix on the page.
Add a code example to the 'Require WAF' section showing either the guardrail definition or a compliant load balancer configuration with WAF attached, consistent with the other guardrail sections.
Add a code example to the 'Require custom domain' section showing a compliant resource configuration, consistent with the other guardrail sections.
optionalImprovements: Add `<ApiReference definitionName="GuardrailDefinition" />` (or the appropriate Props type name from the config schema) near the bottom of the page to give readers a drill-down into exact property names — especially useful for `allowedEngines` values, where the allowed strings are not enumerated in the prose.

Trim the `instanceSize: 'db.t4g.micro'` explanation from the 'Require deletion protection' section — that property is unrelated to deletion protection and the paragraph explaining it is a distraction. The 'Require VPC databases' section already has this explanation; the deletion protection example doesn't need it.

The 'Require dead-letter queue' example hardcodes `deadLetterQueue: 'deadLetterQueue'` as a string reference without explaining this is a resource name reference (not a literal string value). A one-sentence clarification would prevent confusion for first-time readers.

Consider renaming the H2 'How security guardrails work' to a shorter label like 'How guardrails work' — the word 'security' is redundant given the page title, and shorter headings scan better in the ToC.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Source-grounding is accurate throughout — all guardrail type names, property names (enabled, allowedEngines, redrivePolicy, blockedResourceTypes), and resource scoping (require-deletion-protection to relational databases, require-waf to application load balancers, require-dead-letter-queue to SQS queues) correctly match the .d.ts source.

Per-guardrail decision structure (when to enable / when the default is fine / cost tradeoff) is consistent and gives readers enough context to decide, not just configure.

FAQ section is comprehensive and spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS service mechanics, and choosing/comparison questions. The FAQ on deletion protection vs. backups is particularly high-signal.
The 'Combining security guardrails' summary table is a good quick-reference, correctly listing all covered guardrail types in one place.
No forbidden patterns detected: no hand-authored YAML Stacktape config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type imports, no fenced TypeScript blocks containing defineConfig, and no marketing-tone language.
problems: Critical: The page never shows how to actually define a guardrail in stacktape.ts. Every code example shows a compliant resource configuration (e.g., a RelationalDatabase with vpc accessibility) but zero examples show the guardrail definition syntax itself — the `guardrails: [{ type: 'require-vpc-databases', properties: { enabled: true } }]` top-level key and the type+properties structure from the .d.ts source. A developer reading this page knows what the constraints are but cannot implement the guardrails from what is shown.
The require-custom-domain section is the only per-guardrail section with no code example at all — not even a compliant resource config. Every other guardrail section has at least one CodeBlock.

The database-engine-restriction section never shows what the allowedEngines values look like as actual guardrail config — no CodeBlock example, and while the prose mentions `['postgres', 'aurora-postgresql']`, these strings are pulled from nowhere in the source, which only types the property as `string[]` without enumerating valid values.
mandatoryFixes: Add at least one guardrail definition example using <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}> that shows the actual guardrails array syntax in defineConfig — for example, `guardrails: [{ type: 'require-vpc-databases', properties: { enabled: true } }, { type: 'require-deletion-protection', properties: { enabled: true } }]`. Without this, a developer reading the page cannot implement any guardrail. The current examples only show what compliant resources look like, not how to enable the policy. Place this example early in the page — ideally in or after the 'How security guardrails work' section — and reference the type+properties structure from the source types.
optionalImprovements: Add a CodeBlock to the require-custom-domain section showing a hosting bucket or web service with a custom domain configured — every other guardrail section has at least one working example and this section stands out as the only one without.

Qualify the allowedEngines example values ('postgres', 'aurora-postgresql') with a note that valid strings should be verified against the database engine types or schema, since the source types the property only as `string[]` with no enumerated values documented inline.

Consider adding an <ApiReference definitionName='GuardrailDefinition' /> (or the equivalent Props type name) if the component has a schema entry for it — governance pages may not require it, but surfacing the full type would help readers find all available guardrail types without leaving the page.

The 'Combining security guardrails' section mostly restates per-guardrail prose already on the page; consider making it more actionable by adding a combined CodeBlock showing multiple guardrails enabled together in a single defineConfig, which would also address the critical missing-guardrail-syntax problem above.

The FAQ answer for 'What is VPC-only database accessibility?' correctly describes the bastion tunnel pattern, but the bastion host link targets `/resources/security/bastion-host` — verify this route exists before publishing since bastion is not a standard guardrails-adjacent resource page and the URL shape may differ.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, within the target length, and clearly distinguishes preventive policy from runtime monitoring.
H2 headings are compact and mostly noun-form, making the page easy to skim.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the guardrail type names and the high-level distinction between preventive guardrails and reactive alarms, but it contains several Stacktape-specific claims that are broader than the only provided source supports. The main corrections are to avoid over-describing runtime error behavior, to fix the guardrail property model, and to remove or narrow unsupported configuration and operational details.

issues.statement: "Security guardrails use either an `enabled: true` toggle (for `require-*` guardrails) or an allowlist (for restriction guardrails like `database-engine-restriction`)."
issues.reason: The source does not support a two-shape model of enabled toggles or allowlists. Several guardrails use blocklists or numeric limits, including `command-restriction`, `resource-type-restriction`, `database-instance-restriction`, and resource limit guardrails. This wording would mislead readers about how to configure restriction and limit guardrails.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that every guardrail has a `type` and `properties`, and that the property shape depends on the guardrail type: `require-*` guardrails use `enabled`, some restriction guardrails use allowlists, others use blocklists, and limit guardrails use numeric maximums.

issues.statement: The draft repeatedly says violations reject the deployment with an error identifying the offending resource, including: "When a deployment includes a resource that violates an enabled guardrail, the deployment is rejected with an error identifying the offending resource" and "The deployment is rejected with an error identifying which resource violated which policy."
issues.reason: The source defines guardrail types and JSDoc descriptions such as resources being blocked or required, but it does not specify the runtime error format, whether an offending resource is identified, or exact fail-fast behavior. The claim is Stacktape-specific operational behavior that is not grounded in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to sourced behavior: guardrails define policies that block configurations outside the allowed shape. Avoid claiming the exact deployment error contents unless runtime validator or CLI source is provided.

issues.reason: The provided source says `database-engine-restriction` allows database engine types such as `postgres` and `aurora-postgresql`; it does not show version-specific controls. This recommendation depends on a version-granularity claim that is not supported by the guardrail type definition.

evidence.quote: * #### Database engine types that are allowed (e.g., `["postgres", "aurora-postgresql"]`). Others are blocked.

issues.statement: "The `blockedResourceTypes` property accepts a list of Stacktape resource type identifiers — for example, `["open-search-domain", "redis-cluster"]` as shown in the source type definition."
issues.reason: The examples are grounded, but the draft frames them as security examples and says they may be blocked because they are "difficult to secure" or "expose unnecessary attack surface." reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Clear guardrails-vs-alarms distinction backed by a well-structured comparison table — exactly what a reader unfamiliar with AWS governance needs to understand the purpose before diving in.
Consistent 'When to enable / When the default is fine' pattern applied uniformly to every guardrail section — the reader always knows where to find the decision signal.
Working, realistic <CodeBlock intellisense> examples for each guardrail showing a compliant Stacktape configuration, not just abstract documentation.
Strong FAQ with good category mix: Stacktape-specific, underlying-AWS-service, and comparison questions across 10 entries.
Summary combination table at 'Combining security guardrails' is high-signal and citable — engine/tradeoff comparisons in tabular form are the most useful part of a governance page.
Peer H2 structure for 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' is correct — they are independent decisions and the page treats them that way.
The `<Tip>` callout for 'start with these two guardrails' is genuinely opinionated and actionable rather than generic, which fits the target audience well.
problems: The 'Security guardrail definitions' section shows isolated TypeScript object literals (e.g. `{ type: 'require-vpc-databases', properties: { enabled: true } }`) that are not valid standalone TypeScript and cannot be pasted into any file. Guardrails are console-managed, but the section heading and code formatting look like a config recipe to a first-time reader. There is no prose saying 'this is illustrative only — you cannot place these in stacktape.ts'. A beginner will be confused about what to do with these snippets.

The claim that `require-vpc-databases` accepts accessibility modes `'vpc'` and `'scoping-workloads-in-vpc'` is not grounded in guardrails.d.ts. The source only states 'all databases must use VPC-only accessibility' without specifying which accessibility mode values satisfy the guardrail. These mode identifiers come from the relational-database types, not the guardrail source — if the guardrail runtime validates a different set of modes, the docs would be wrong.

The claim that `require-vpc-databases` specifically covers OpenSearch domains is not directly supported by the guardrail source, which says 'all databases' without naming resource types. The page asserts OpenSearch is in scope in both the section prose and the FAQ without grounding.

The `//` comment labels inside the TypeScript fenced block ('// require-* guardrails — enabled toggle', '// Restriction guardrails — allowlist') function as section dividers rather than code the user would type. While this doesn't technically violate the shell-specific `#` comment rule, the comments land in the copy buffer and make the snippet look like structured example code when it is only illustrative.

'How security guardrails work' is a 5-word sentence-form H2. Style guidance recommends short noun-form labels (1-5 words). The current heading is at the boundary; 'How guardrails work' or just 'Overview' would be tighter.
mandatoryFixes: Reframe the 'Security guardrail definitions' section so a first-time reader understands these object literals are illustrative shapes — not config code. Add a sentence explicitly stating 'These definitions are entered in the Stacktape Console, not in your stacktape.ts file.' Alternatively, replace the raw fenced block entirely with a prose description and a link to the Console guardrails page, since no standalone code snippet can demonstrate console-managed configuration.

Remove or qualify the specific accessibility mode values (`'vpc'` and `'scoping-workloads-in-vpc'`) from the require-vpc-databases section and from the FAQ, because guardrails.d.ts does not enumerate which values satisfy the guardrail. Replace with source-grounded phrasing: 'the database must be configured for VPC-only access — see the relational database page for the supported accessibility modes' with a link.

Either remove the OpenSearch domain claim from both the require-vpc-databases section body and the FAQ answer (since guardrails.d.ts is silent on which resource types are covered), or source it from a runtime validator that explicitly lists the covered resource types. If unsourced, write narrowly: 'databases configured with public accessibility are blocked' without naming specific resource types the guardrail does or does not cover.

The code block should contain only the object literals a reader might reference, not explanatory labels that land in the copy buffer.
Shorten 'How security guardrails work' to 'How guardrails work' or 'Overview' to bring the H2 in line with the 1-5 word noun-form convention.

The 'Security guardrail definitions' fenced block currently lists only 6 of the 15 guardrail types. Add a note that the 6 shown are the security-focused subset and that the full 15-type list appears on the guardrails overview page, so a reader doesn't assume the list is exhaustive.

Consider adding a short 'Error output' example (just the GUARDRAIL error message shape) in the 'How guardrails work' section — a first-time reader benefits from knowing what the failure looks like before they've ever triggered one, so it's not a surprise when it happens.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and close to the required 40-75 word range.
Headings are compact and scannable, with useful separation between preventive guardrails and reactive alarms.
